notes: Official Treasury product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Since you're working at Microsoft, your freelance income is "stacking" on top of your existing salary, which means it will likely be taxed at your highest marginal rate plus self-employment tax. 

To prevent a surprise bill (or penalty) next April, you should focus on three things: setting aside the right percentage, tracking your "home office" and other expenses, and deciding how to pay the IRS.

### 1. Set aside 30–40% of every freelance check
Because you have a high W-2 salary, your freelance income is subject to two separate taxes:
*   **Income Tax:** Since you're likely in a higher bracket (e.g., `24%` or `32%`), every dollar of freelance profit is taxed at that rate.
*   **Self-Employment (SE) Tax:** You owe an additional `15.3%` to cover the employer and employee halves of Social Security and Medicare.
    *   *Note:* Since your Microsoft salary may already be near the Social Security wage base (`$184,500` for 2026), you might only owe the `2.9%` Medicare portion of the SE tax on your side income. 

### 2. Track "Business" expenses to lower the bill
You only pay tax on your **net profit** (Income minus Expenses). Common deductions for freelancers include:
*   **Home Office:** If you have a dedicated space in your Seattle home used *exclusively* for freelance work, you can deduct `$5 per square foot` (up to 300 sq ft) using the simplified method.
*   **Software & Hardware:** Any tools, subscriptions, or equipment used for your freelance projects.
*   **Professional Services:** If you hire a bookkeeper or pay for tax prep software, that's deductible.
*   **QBI Deduction:** You may be eligible to deduct an additional `20%` of your net freelance income from your income taxes (though this doesn't reduce the SE tax).

### 3. Handle the "Pay-As-You-Go" requirement
The IRS requires taxes to be paid as you earn the money. If you expect to owe more than `$1,000` from your freelance work, you have two options:
*   **Quarterly Estimated Payments:** Send a check or pay online to the IRS four times a year.
*   **Adjust your Microsoft Withholding:** This is often easier. You can update your W-4 at work to withhold an extra fixed amount from each Microsoft paycheck to cover your freelance tax liability. This counts as "paid evenly" throughout the year, which can help avoid underpayment penalties even if you start late.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Open a separate bank account.** Even a simple checking account at a different bank helps keep your "business" income and expenses separate from your personal life. It makes tax time significantly faster and provides a clear audit trail if the IRS ever asks questions.

---

### Your Freelance To-Do List
- [ ] **Calculate your "Safe Harbor" target:** Check your 2025 tax return. If you pay in `110%` of last year's total tax through withholding, you won't owe a penalty regardless of how much you earn freelancing.
- [ ] **Separate your funds:** Move your recent freelance deposit into a dedicated "Tax/Business" savings account.
- [ ] **Document your home office:** Measure the square footage of your dedicated workspace to use the simplified deduction.
- [ ] **Decide on your payment method:** Will you use IRS Direct Pay for quarterly payments, or update your W-4 at Microsoft?
